Station_Description: Hannah Shoal subsurface ecosystem mooring with a bunch of sensros and benthic webcam; Seabird 37sm, SBE SEAPHox, Contros HYDRO-C, Satlantic SUNA, Hydrobios, Aural Sequoia LISST, Teledyne ADCP, Aanderaa RCM9,
Variable_Names: sea_water_temperature (35m, 48m, near-bottom), sea_water_electrical_conductivity ( ~35m, ~48m, near-bottom), sea_water_practical_salinity (35m, 48m, near-bottom), current_velocity (3-30 m, 1m bins; near-bottom), sea_surface_wave_significant_height, sea_surface_wave_to_direction, sea_surface_wave_mean_period, nitrate (35m), dissolved_oxygen (35 m), sea_water_pH_reported_on_total_scale (35 m), partial_pressure_of_carbon_dioxide_in_sea_water (35 m), sediment_trap_parameters (38m), fish_and_plankton_acoustic_profile (0.5-30m in 4 cm bins), partical_size_spectra (35 m), sea_water_pressure (35, ~48 m, near bottom), downwelling_photosynthetic_photon_flux_in_sea_water
Additional_notes: two to three moorings at the CEO which are rotated annually.  ~1 year lag on data ingestion. NOTE: Depths of sensors may vary from year to year, and started out shallower around 27m in the first year on the CEO1...the depths have increased a bit to avoid ice and as they rotate the mooring and the exact water depths for each year are reported in the ERDDAP. NOT SURE OF CF NAMES for all these variables as it depends on the units;  Not sure what sediment trap parameters are being reported;  not sure CF name for fish and plankton acoustics; not sure CF name for partical size.  These data are being processed in the workspace…but not all reporting to the AOOS Portal. (KB: Don't worry about CF names, I'll figure it out). We are still working on how to best host all variations these data which are being stored on the NPRB Workspace...data curerntly not showing on the AOOS Portal are likely to made into a catalogue item where it is downloadable, but not visualized. This poses complications for Axiom, but I am working on it. NPRB is the Data Managing entity.

Variable_Names: sea_water_temperature, sea_water_electrical_conductivity, sea_water_practical_salinity,  sea_water_pressure,  near bottom), downwelling_photosynthetic_photon_flux_in_sea_water
Additional_notes: Data visualizaed on the AOOS Portal includes 2 CTDS  on the CEO1 mooring, deployed between 27 and 42 m and PAR. Other parameters maybe available from year to year. You can visualized the data by depth (scroll the depths using DEVICES icon): AOOS Portal link: https://portal.aoos.org/#metadata/123828/station/data

Variable_Names: sea_water_temperature, sea_water_electrical_conductivity, sea_water_practical_salinity,  sea_water_turbidity, fluorescence,  fractional_saturation_of_oxygen_in_sea_water,  sea_water_pressure, downwelling_photosynthetic_photon_flux_in_sea_water
Additional_notes: This is the list of all sensors reporting to the AOOS Portal are at ~33m on this data feed, though there maybe other sensors on this mooring as well not reported on the portal. AOOS Portal link: https://portal.aoos.org/#metadata/123829/station/data

Station_Description: Surface real time mooring: CDOM, 150kHz ADCP, SBE 37 TSP, ITP (Profiling CTD_ conductivity, temperature, pressure, salinity, depth, Chlorophyll, CDOM, OBS, DO, NO3), GPS, wind speed, wind direction, air temp, relative humidity, dew point, barometric pressure,  downwelling photosynthetic photon flux in sea water
Currently_Operational: Y - but not real time at moment, so data not updated as of 12/2019
Platform_Funder: M.J. Murdock Charitable Trust, AOOS, NPRB, Gulf Watch (Exxom Valdez Oil Spill Trustees Council) , UAF, NSF-LTER, others
Variable_Names: Real time mooring: 150kHz ADCP (205m), SBE 37 TSP (205m), ITP (Profiling CTD:  sea_water_electrical_conductivity, sea_water_temperature, sea_water_pressure, sea_water_practical_salinity, depth, mass_concentration_of_chlorophyll_in_sea_water, CDOM, OBS, DO, NO3); GPS, wind_speed, wind_from_direction, air_temperature, relative_humidity, dew_point_temperature, air_pressure,  downwelling_photosynthetic_photon_flux_in_sea_water (1m),  mass_concentration_of_chlorophyll_in_sea_water (1m)
Additional_notes: The buoy was not serving real time data feeds from the profiling CTD the ADCP current meter. The Surface MET sensors and the 1 m fluorometer and PAR were reporting data realtime in 2019. Will work on WMO assignments if and when this mooring becomes fully operational. This buoy stopped reporting real time data in Dec of 2019.

Station_Long_Name: Alyeska Resort, Top of Glacier Bowl Express
Additional_notes: cross checked and correctied.  cf says preciptation_amount, "Amount" means mass per unit area. "Precipitation" in the earth's atmosphere means precipitation of water in all phases.  Axiom is using the non-cf name 'precipitation_increment' to describe precipitation values reported as a rate of accumulation measured as length over time, e.g. 'mm/hr'. What we call 'precipitation (accumulation)' maps to the cf-name 'lwe_thickness_of_precipitation_amount' doesn't contain a time component, though we create something like that for each time-step or bin of values we show in the portal.

Data_Manager: Stillwater Technoloiges, LLC, NOAA Alaska-Pacific River Forecast Center (APRFC)
Additional_notes: Deering WL station stopped reporting on Oct 4, 2025 during a storm. Plans are to replace this staion in 2026. This station was originally installed in September 2018 but failed. It was not reinstalled until June of 2021 so was not on the 2019-2020 RA Asset Inventories. Data can also be found at the NOAA APRFC website: https://water.weather.gov/ahps2/hydrograph.php?wfo=pafg3&gage=drta2

Install completed end of Nov 2021, but non-iced data not reporting until March 2022. There are two water level gauges using two different technologies currently deployed at Dillingham, AK — Gauge 1 and Guage 2. Having two gauges is typical practice for long term stations so there is redundancy in the observations in order to reduce the potential for data gaps.Despite this redundancy, Dillingham is an extremely challenging site since the dock goes dry, making data gaps still likely and expected, even with redundant technologies. Gauge 1 is a bubbler system secured to the seawall. It goes dry during spring tides, and also can freeze-up during winter. Gauge 2, is a downward looking radar that is co-located with Gauge 1, and also secured to the seawall. During winter, ice fast to the seawall can block the radar's view of the water, invalidating measurements until after the ice goes away. For both gauges, care should be taken to verify data collected during the months of October through April.

Additional_notes: This Stillwater Technologies iGage began collecting data on July 24, 2020. It was installed on a wood post pier. Station_Description: Kotzebue on Stevens Way Bridge  Water Level (Tier C)
Data_Manager: Stillwater Technologies, NOAA Alaska-Pacific River Forecast Center (APRFC)
Additional_notes: This station has had multiple previous deployments between Nov 16, 2016 and Jun 28, 2021. On June 8, 2022, a field team traveled to Kotzebue to replace the sensor with a Stillwater Technologies iGageX and re-establish the site. Keith Horen computed tidal datums using the Alaska Today Datum Portal, which are available in the linked Station Definition Form.On Nov. 3, 2022, ortho and station datum heights in the metadata file were updated. Data from 6/02/2022 to 11/3/2022 were reloaded. Data are also found on NOAA APRFC website:  https://water.weather.gov/ahps2/hydrograph.php?wfo=pafg2&gage=kzta2

Data_Manager: NOAA Water Resources Regions, NWS (Owner, Publisher), NOAA Alaska-Pacific River Forecast Center (APRFC) (Affiliate)
Additional_notes: This system (JUDD Sensor) Stopped reporting in fall 2021, and has not been fixed. Data started to look noisy in mid-summer, but sensor appeared to bounce back intermittantly until October 2021 when it stopped report. Data reported Height data only goes to April 2018. Water Surface above Datum data runs thru October 2021. A Stillwater Technologies iGage was installed on on Sept 13, 2022 to replace  Judd Sensor. Two other sensors were previously installed at this site and failed. Site re-established 07/21/2017 and again 08/08/2019.Data used to be processed and accessed thru NOAA Water Resources Regions, National Weather Service, a federall service, so it had to go through them. Normally this is a federal dataset not reported on this sheet, but because we help fund the hardware and installation, we include it. The AOOS portal still hosts data from APRFC but Axiom is also now pulling data directly from the Stillwater Technologies site (we are working out which one is better to use for data streaming). The two sources are reported as separate station pages at the moment, but are reporting same data.

Station_Description: Orion Space Solutions GNSS-Reflectoometry water level
Additional_notes: The Orion sensor was discontinued in Dec 2025. AOOS is having the receiver replaced by JOA to full GNSS-R in 2026. This will be reported as a new station altogether as it might be relocated. AOOS owns the sensor purchased from Orion Space Solutions. These data are experimental water level derivations using satellite data. Orion Space Solutions provides Axiom with the derived water level measurements directly. Raw data are displayed as the default, and are plotted relative to the GPS antenna (not leveled with respect to MLLW). The corrected data can be displayed by toggling Devices to MLLW above the chart. JOA Surveys provided the MLLW for leveling for the corrected data based on five benchmarks in the area.

Station_Description: Englullrarmiut offshore western Nunavik Island, AKSeasonal Wave Buoy
Platform_Funder: Alaska Coastal Cooperative (ACC), NSF ACTION Project Buoy
Platform_Maintainer: Alaska Coastal Cooperative (ACC), University of Alaska
Variable_Names: sea_water_temperature, sea_surface_wave_mean_period, sea_surface_wave_directional_spread,  sea_surface_wave_period_at_variance_spectral_density_maximum, sea_surface_wave_significant_height, sea_surface_wave_from_direction, wind_speed, wind_from_direction
Additional_notes: These are seasonally deployed wave buoys as part of a University of Alaska, Alaska Coastal Cooperative project with NSF (ACTION). AOOS and Tetra Tech (Formerly Axiom Data Science) are partners on this project for data management. Deployments also occurred in 2024, but these data were not uploaded to the AOOS Data Portal until 2025. Future deployments will likely be called out as separate stations, due to locational differences from year to year. These station deployments likely will need to updated and changed annually. This buoy broke from its mooring in November and became a drifter. Data for the drifter component of this deployment is currently being added to the AOOS Data Portal and will be added to 2026 ASSET INVENTORY.  list of all UAF ACC deployments here: https://portal.aoos.org/#metadata/2398/sensor_source

Station_Description: Juneau, AK Ambient Marine Water Quality Continuous Monitoring in Harbors
Variable_Names: air_pressure, sea_water_electrical_conductivity, mass_concentration_of_oxygen_in_sea_water, fractional_saturation_of_oxygen_in_sea_water, sea_water_practical_salinity, sea_water_density, specific_conductance, sea_water_temperature, total_dissolved_solids, sea_water_turbidity, sea_water_ph_reported_on_total_scale,
Additional_notes: These sensors in the harbors of Seward, Juneau and Ketchikan are managed by the Alaska Department of Environmental Conservation. Sensors are at approximately 1 meter depth. Measurements are taken every half hour and uploaded every four hours. Verification checks and cleaning occur at least twice annually. Data presented in graphs are near real-time and have not undergone Quality Control (QC) procedures.

Station_Description: This beachfront camera on Oak Island, NC is being used to monitor beach hazards and coastal change, including the evolution of beach nourishment projects.
Additional_notes: camera

Station_Description: This beachfront camera on Oak Island, NC is being used to monitor beach hazards and coastal change, including the evolution of beach nourishment projects.
Additional_notes: camera

Station_Long_Name: Beachfront from Hampton Inn, Corolla, NC
Station_Description: This camera is located at a Hampton Inn in Corolla, NC. Currituck County partnered with the WebCOOS team to install this camera to monitor, assess, and report on beach hazards and activities. Live beach footage is available to the public to view from their desktop and mobile devices.
Additional_notes: camera

Station_Long_Name: Beachfront from Sailfish Street Beach Access, Corolla, NC
Station_Description: This camera is located at the Sailfish Street Public Beach Access in Corolla, NC. Currituck County partnered with the WebCOOS team to install this camera to monitor, assess, and report on beach hazards and activities. Live beach footage is available to the public to view from their desktop and mobile devices.
Additional_notes: camera

Station_Description: This camera is positioned at the NOAA NOS CO-OPS tide gauge located at the Duke University Marine Lab in Beaufort, NC. It is a partnership between CO-OPS, the Duke Marine Lab, and the WebCOOS team to monitor water levels and coastal flooding.
Additional_notes: camera

Station_Description: This camera is positioned on the Charleston Branch Pilots pier overlooking Charleston Harbor, the Cooper River and nearby infrastructure, and is co-located with the nearby NOAA NOS CO-OPS tide gauge. It is a partnership between CO-OPS, the Charleston Branch Pilots and the WebCOOS team to monitor sea state and coastal flooding.
Additional_notes: camera

Station_Long_Name: Jennette's Pier (north view), Nags Head, NC
Station_Description: This camera is located on Jennette's Pier in Nags Head, NC. It is being used to inform rip current detection models.
Additional_notes: camera

Station_Long_Name: Jennette's Pier (south view), Nags Head, NC
Station_Description: This camera is located on Jennette's Pier in Nags Head, NC. It is being used to inform rip current detection models.
Additional_notes: camera

Station_Description: This camera overlooks Masonboro Inlet situated between Wrightsville Beach, NC and Masonboro Island, NC. It is being used to monitor sea state conditions and potential shoaling issues within the inlet.
Additional_notes: camera

Station_Long_Name: North Inlet-Winyah Bay National Estuarine Research Reserve Dock, Georgetown, SC
Station_Description: This camera is being used for bird counts and monitoring flooding at the North Inlet-Winyah Bay National Estuarine Research Reserve. This camera is coupled with a water level sensor.
Additional_notes: camera

Station_Long_Name: University of North Carolina Wilmington Dock (north view), Wilmington, NC
Station_Description: This camera is located on a dock at the Center for Marine Science at the University of North Carolina Wilmington. It is provided for general viewing purposes, but the WebCOOS team is evaluating the potential for tracking vessel activity.
Additional_notes: camera

Station_Long_Name: University of North Carolina Wilmington Dock (south view), Wilmington, NC
Station_Description: This camera is located on a dock at the Center for Marine Science at the University of North Carolina Wilmington. It is provided for general viewing purposes, but the WebCOOS team is evaluating the potential for tracking vessel activity.
Additional_notes: camera

Station_Description: This camera on Folly Beach, SC is being used to monitor beach use.
Additional_notes: camera

Station_Long_Name: Rosemont Community, Peace Street, Charleston, SC (1)
Station_Description: This camera is activated when rain is imminent and is used to inform the community of the scope of community flooding including roads, homes, and community green spaces. This camera is coupled with a rainfall sensor and an air quality monitor.
Additional_notes: camera

Station_Long_Name: South Carolina Maritime Museum, Georgetown, SC
Station_Description: This camera at the South Carolina Maritime Museum is being used to monitor river use on the Sampit River, people traffic at events, and to remotely evaluate flooding impacts during storms. This camera is coupled with a water level sensor and an air quality monitor.
Additional_notes: camera

Station_Long_Name: Sunny Day Flooding Project, Carolina Beach camera #1, Carolina Beach, NC
Station_Description: This camera is part of a network of sensors deployed on land to understand the frequency and causes of coastal floods, particularly those that occur outside of extreme events like hurricanes. The cameras are located in flood hotspots identified by community members. Flood imagery and co-located water level measurements are used to identify flooded roadways for risk communication and to validate numerical models that can be used to test flood mitigation strategies desired by communities.
Additional_notes: camera

Station_Long_Name: Madeira Beach, FL USGS CoastCam Camera 1
Station_Description: This camera in Madeira Beach, FL, is a USGS CoastCam and is being used to study coastal water levels at the Madeira Beach. Additional information about and imagery products from the camera are available on the USGS camera website.
Additional_notes: camera

Station_Long_Name: Madeira Beach, FL USGS CoastCam Camera 2
Station_Description: This camera in Madeira Beach, FL, is a USGS CoastCam and is being used to study coastal water levels at the Madeira Beach. Additional information about and imagery products from the camera are available on the USGS camera website.
Additional_notes: camera
